sections.heading: Problem Statement
sections.content:
The graph view is the primary way a developer sees the health of their codebase at a glance. It renders every indexed node as a Cytoscape.js element with type-colored bodies and staleness-status rings, giving a visual map of what is documented, what is stale, and how nodes connect.

Despite being implemented and working, two gaps make it hard to trust and use for staleness triage:

**Stale count is invisible.** The sidebar shows total node count but no 'N stale' summary. A developer cannot tell at a glance how many nodes need attention, and the number they see in the graph cannot be compared to what `cortex check` or the MCP `cortex_check` tool reports.

**Two-tier staleness creates transient inconsistency.** The initial `/api/all` load uses a fast mtime-based check that returns `NEEDS_RESCAN` for changed files — not `DESC_STALE`. The rich hash-based check (`/api/check`) runs in the background and overwrites the map after load. During this window, a developer counting yellow-ringed nodes gets a different number than `cortex_check` would report.

**Doc section nodes inflate the check count but are hard to see in the graph.** `cortex check` counts all node types including `doc_file` and `doc_section`. In many projects, the majority of stale nodes are doc sections whose linked code changed. These nodes are valid entries in the graph but cluster visually near their parent and their parent/section relationship is not obvious, making it hard to understand what is stale and why.

sections.heading: User Stories
sections.content:
**US-GV-01 — See project health at a glance**
As a developer, I want the graph view to show me type-colored nodes with staleness rings so I can visually understand the shape of my project and which nodes need attention.

**US-GV-02 — Filter the graph by node type**
As a developer, I want checkboxes in the sidebar to show/hide node types (atomic_process, composite_process, document, constraint) so I can declutter the graph and focus on code nodes or doc nodes independently.

**US-GV-03 — Filter the graph by staleness status**
As a developer, I want radio buttons in the sidebar to filter the graph to nodes with a specific staleness status so I can see only what needs work.

**US-GV-04 — See a stale node count that matches `cortex check`**
As a developer, I want the sidebar to show a live count of non-CLEAN nodes broken down by type (e.g. '3 code stale · 1 coverage stale · 2 content stale') that matches what `cortex check` reports, so I can trust the dashboard number without cross-referencing the CLI.

**US-GV-05 — Understand why a node is stale**
As a developer, I want to click a stale node and see in the detail panel whether it is CODE_STALE, DESC_STALE, CONTENT_STALE, COVERAGE_STALE, or STRUCTURAL_DRIFT, so I know what action to take.

**US-GV-06 — Select layout algorithm**
As a developer, I want to choose between layout algorithms (cose, dagre, breadthfirst, grid) so I can pick the layout that best reveals the current graph's structure.

**US-GV-07 — Filter by edge type**
As a developer, I want to toggle edge types (composes, calls, validates, documents, covers) so I can see only the dependency relationships relevant to my current task.

**US-GV-08 — Rescan without leaving the graph**
As a developer, I want a Rescan button in the graph toolbar that triggers a cortex build and refreshes the graph in place so I can see the updated staleness state after editing code.

**US-GV-09 — Re-check staleness without rebuilding**
As a developer, I want a Check button that re-reads source files and updates staleness rings in place — without triggering a full rebuild — so I can see whether code I just edited is stale without waiting for an index rebuild.

**US-GV-10 — No transient NEEDS_RESCAN state on graph load**
As a developer, I want the graph view initial load to derive staleness from the history table so that no node ever shows a transient NEEDS_RESCAN state and the staleness counts match `cortex check` immediately on load — with no background re-check required.

sections.heading: Current Capabilities
sections.content:
| Capability | Status |
|---|---|
| Cytoscape.js graph render with all nodes and edges | Done |
| Node type coloring (atomic_process, composite_process, doc_file, doc_section) | Done |
| Staleness rings on nodes (DESC_STALE=orange, STRUCTURAL_DRIFT=red, VERIFIED=green border) | Done |
| Node type checkboxes in sidebar | Done |
| Staleness radio filter in sidebar | Done |
| Edge type toggle checkboxes | Done |
| Edge type filter bug fixed (bypass style conflict with filterNodes) | Done |
| Layout selector — cose (force) and dagre (hierarchy) | Done |
| Layout animation disabled (instant placement) | Done |
| Rescan button (POST /api/rescan + full graph refresh) | Done |
| Check button (read-only hash-based staleness refresh, no rebuild) | Done |
| Large project mode (>400 nodes → defaults to list view) | Done |
| Step edge labels toggle | Done |
| Node click → neighborhood graph + detail panel | Done |
| Additional layout algorithms (breadthfirst, grid, concentric) | Not started |
| Stale count summary in sidebar (N code stale · N doc stale) | Not started |
| History-table-based initial staleness (no transient NEEDS_RESCAN) | Not started |
| Stale node explanation in detail panel (code changed at X, doc last updated at Y) | Not started |

sections.heading: Staleness Count Discrepancy
sections.content:
The mismatch between what `cortex check` (or the MCP `cortex_check` tool) reports and what the graph shows comes from three compounding factors:

**1. Fast path vs rich path on initial load**
`/api/all` runs `_compute_staleness()` — an mtime-based scan — and returns `NEEDS_RESCAN` for changed files, not `DESC_STALE`. The rich path (`/api/check` → `_compute_staleness_rich()`) fires as a background request after load and overwrites the staleness map. Until that request completes, graph nodes show `NEEDS_RESCAN` rings instead of `DESC_STALE`. A user counting yellow rings during this window gets a lower or different number than the eventual rich-path count.

**2. Doc section node inflation**
`cortex check` counts every node type: `atomic_process`, `composite_process`, `doc_file`, and `doc_section`. In many projects, the majority of stale nodes are doc sections — e.g. a doc file with 10 sections where each section links to a recently-changed function produces 11 stale nodes (1 doc_file + 10 doc_sections). These nodes appear in the graph but are visually smaller and cluster near their parent doc node, making them hard to count individually.

**3. No aggregate stale count in the graph UI**
There is no number in the dashboard that says 'N nodes are non-CLEAN'. The only way to count is to apply the DESC_STALE staleness filter radio and observe how many nodes remain — but this only works after the rich path has run and does not distinguish code stale from doc stale.

**Recommended fix direction (see Backlog):**
- Show a staleness summary above the filter radio buttons: 'DESC_STALE: 10 code · 11 doc'
- Block the staleness filter UI (or show a loading indicator) until the background rich check completes, so users never interact with NEEDS_RESCAN as a final state
- Long-term: run the rich path synchronously on first load; use mtime fast path only for background refresh after rescan

sections.heading: Backlog
sections.content:
**History-table-based initial staleness load (US-GV-10)** — Replace the `/api/all` call to `_compute_staleness()` (mtime fast path) with `_compute_staleness_from_history()` — a pure DB read that derives status from the last `node_history` row per node. Eliminates NEEDS_RESCAN as a visible state, makes initial graph consistent with `cortex check`, removes all file I/O from the render path. Remove the background `/api/check` auto-fire on page load. The Check button retains full hash-based re-read for catching changes made to disk after the last build.

**Staleness summary widget (US-GV-04)** — Show a compact staleness breakdown above the filter radio buttons: e.g. 'CODE_STALE: 3 · COVERAGE_STALE: 1 · CONTENT_STALE: 2'. Clicking a count activates that staleness filter. Depends on history-table-based initial load so the count is accurate on first render.

**Staleness count per filter option** — In the sidebar staleness radio list, show counts per status (e.g. 'CODE_STALE (3)'). Makes it clear how many nodes match each filter without having to apply it.

**Doc section staleness drill-down** — When a doc_file node is CONTENT_STALE, the detail panel should list which sections are stale and which code nodes they link to. Currently requires opening the Doc Manager to find this.
